speaker: interviewer
text: --95. And we are in Sydney, Australia. And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pepa, you were telling us about some incidents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I come home and was sitting at the window, which, as I told you, looked over a road which didn't run to the ghetto anymore. We were the first house, first wall of the ghetto. And there I saw a man with a white band going from the opposite side approaching the ghetto. A Jew. But then I recognized him. He was a retarded man. As long as I live, I remember him being in the town. You know, every town has its own funny people, and he was a retarded man. But he was very strong, so people were using him as a carrier. And now, at this moment, he was carrying a cupboard, going towards the ghetto. Probably somebody, you know, wanted to bring some furniture from outside. And he was on the road, very close, when suddenly, a German came in sight. He did not hesitate a moment. He took a gun out, and he shot him. And the poor man fell facedown, and the cupboard on top of him. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a few days, two or three days later, they walked in again. And they walked, as I told you, and we went through. But as my father did not have-- was still in Wehrmacht, but now they started-- the Wehrmacht started to release the older people, and he was not covered.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as for the-- after this one, they told us to move closer to the center of this area which they decided to give us. So we have to leave those-- this part and go behind it. Somehow we found a room, a room for only for us, for-- for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was situated on the corner-- this is very important, because you will see afterwards why I describe that. There was a house, a few floors. But the room was on the street level on the corner. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And by this time, I went out with my mother for work. We were cleaning out the places which-- which the Jews left. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was our job. And a group-- a whole group, and we were coming back in the evening. My father was inside the ghetto, because he did not work by then. And my husband went in the morning to the-- with the group to the Wehrmacht and back. Then one day, they kept the Wehrmacht people in the barracks, so we knew that's going to happen again. And they did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they moved in. This time, they took a lot of people. And we were-- was-- my father had to find a hiding place.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not have. So he did. He was hidden. My husband was at the Wehrmacht, and we were at the working group. So they cleaned up the ghetto, and again-- and they pushed us even further. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So by that time, the new ghetto-- we saw that it is the last part. Because it was surrounded by a fence, and it was on the gate. Entrance gate. One entrance gate, which was always under surveillance-- there was the guard standing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what was it-- it was just across the street from our room. That was the entrance gate to the new ghetto. Somehow my father, who was in the ghetto because we were all working, found a place in the new space. That was the oldest and the poorest part of the-- of Przemysl. That was a part which I-- I don't think that I saw ever before. They were only the very small houses. There was practically no water anywhere. Running water doesn't-- didn't exist. Anyway, he found the room. But the room was, in no time, filled up. So we ended up with 20 people-- strangers-- in the-- in this room. We were still going to work. My husband and my father was inside the ghetto. Yes. Then one day, it was very-- it was very hard, because it was then they were really very strict about what you bring into the ghetto, you see. We were outside, so the Poles were approaching us, buying and selling, you know, stuff. But now, it was very hard and very bad. And as there was no-- practically no water and no food, it started to get really, very hard. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But then when we were in the new-- this-- the new space, my husband sent us a message. He left in the room a wallet with money, which was not so important. It was important, but not.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he left some false papers, which he procured for himself. And that was dangerous. How we can get it out?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And now I had the idea-- which I think today the most silly idea, but then it was the only one that I had. I told my mother, look. We are passing this room everyday going to work and from work. When we will go next day from work, I will jump into the room. It was just-- the-- the entrance is to the corner. I will-- now I know where the wallet is. Because I would have taken before, but I did not know where it is. That he has it. That I will take the wallet. And you tell the leader to slow down, and I will join you before we go into the ghetto. And so we did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came close to the house. I went from the ranks straight into the room. I took the wallet, and I took a few-- this-- underwear which we washed the day before. It was hanging, you know, before we left. It was still-- winter underwear-- it was still wet. And I made-- made a bundle, and the wallet in it, and then run out. I am running out, nobody's there. I was left on the road outside the ghetto, and I did not know what to do. I was just standing there, and suddenly, I see an SS man approaching. Tall, young, immaculately dressed, like they were then. And he approached.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I could not run. I was just standing there, in the same position as the poor old man then was. Outside the ghetto, with my-- we have a white band and the bundle in my hand. And I was just waiting until he reaches for the gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he must have been as astounded as I was. [LAUGHS] Something-- a vision like that, he would not expect. So he was standing still.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for me, it was an eternity. I don't know how long it was. He asked me, what are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I explained to him just as it was. I left out only the story about the wallet. I told him, look.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we lived here, and we were-- when we had to move, I was with my mother at work. We could not move anything. Now winter is approaching, and we needed a few winter things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just-- exactly as it was, without the wallet. And he looks at me. And he tells me, 'wait with me here, because my group is coming back to the ghetto, and I will take you with the group into the ghetto.' Until today, I can't understand his reaction.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They were trained, yes? To be killers. Like the other was, with the poor man, yes? I could not-- until today, I go over and over this incident. I see myself standing there-- small, thin, nervous, neglected-- because we had no water even to wash ourselves. And what he thought-- perhaps he thought that, why he has to dirty his hands? Sooner-- rather sooner than later, I will be killed anyway. I don't know what he thought.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, that was his reaction when I told him my story-- 'wait with me.' You could see the faces of those Jewish men when they come and saw me standing there with the-- with the SS man in the middle of the road. But anyway, another incident which finished happily for me. A few days later, they called up all the women. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they started to choose those who will get the working pass. My mother got. I didn't. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The 18th of November, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had to move in. We knew about it. Luckily for me, a cousin of mine-- she is on one of those photos-- she had a pass, and she decided to go to the-- to the other side. She had a Polish friend somewhere. So she left me her pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: With her name on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: With her name on it. Which I had to repeat-- because, you see, that is, again, the Jewish story. We knew her as [? Solomon, ?] and her real name was [? Pfeffercorn, ?] and I was not used to it. I had to repeat, repeat in my mind, so I will react to it at once if somebody calls [? Pfeffercorn. ?] Anyway, she-- she went over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We never heard about her. She never-- she did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she get across?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went over the-- over or under the fence, I don't know. Somehow she went. And we did not know the name of the friend. We did not know even where to look and what to look. She did not survive. But she gave me my life. She gave me the pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the 18th of November '42, they gather us-- all those people who had to passes, of course. And my husband was, again, at the Wehrmacht. All the other, now a big number, were hidden. And we were arranged in a square.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the officer has a bundle-- sheets of paper-- and he started to read names. So those names he read, they went to the side. They were to live. And suddenly, in the middle of it, a small child, a boy, ran out of our ranks. And the mother, in panic, after him. And he just shot them like that in front of us and-- And that he reads on, and suddenly he stopped. That's it. He did not come to the end of the alphabet. That was-- I think he finished at P-- not-- not even at P, because he did not come to my name. And so he told, that's it. So my mother was standing there between the living. And she looked at me, and she could not help. She could not. And suddenly, another soldier comes to him and hands him a new sheet of paper and told him, you forgot that. And he started to read some more names, and mine was in between. The-- [? Pfeffercorn. ?] And so I went with my mother. That was the 18th of November. The next day-- ah, in the evening, after the roundup, that time, I think, only a few thousand were left. So it means we, in the ghetto, the legitimate survivors, were only a few hundred. But those who came from the hiding and from the Wehrmacht together-- there were a few thousand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that evening, my husband came with his group from the Wehrmacht, because the roundup is over, so they come home. And he told us that my younger brother, Henry, is in town with papers-- a German-issued Polish Kennkarte, identity card, for me. And he wants to take me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must mention here that this brother was never in the ghetto. He did not look Jewish. He was tall. He was blond. He was absolutely different than we were. He was tall, blond, blue-eyed, white-skinned. He spoke perfect German. And he was a-- a typical Aryan that Hitler could not even dream of. So he was not-- never in there. And he was in touch with us here and there, especially with my brother. Because he could walk into the Wehrmacht compound and back. Especially the way he wore boots and the leather coat-- he looked like somebody. Now, we are sitting there in the evening and talking about it. And I did not want to leave my parents, and I did not fancy my chances on the outside. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, I did not think about it at all, because I did not want to leave them alone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then my father looked at us, and he decided. He told, you have to go. Because you saw yesterday, where you were standing then together, that you can't help each other. That each one of you could go to your death without the help of the other. So you go and try to live. Someone has to survive, if you can.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went. The next-- we saw the logic of it. We were all doomed. And we never knew how, and when, and this. So I left in the morning when my husband, with his group, went back to work. I was with them. And when we approached the house where my-- this brother was waiting for me, I took off my white band, give it-- gave it to them, and I entered the house. I entered to the side of the living. Nobody saw you go in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was a narrow street. It was a side street. The group was quite big, you know. And I just walked in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I walked in into the flat where my brother was, there were quite a few people. The hostess, a-- a young woman, and quite a few people. And they greeted me like one of them. You see? Nobody even took-- the hostess knew, of course, what for I come there, you see? Did you look different? Did you smell? Did you-- were you dirty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I was not very clean. Not very clean. Look, we lived as we could. We could-- we had to even look clean, present-- present ourselves decently when we-- went went to work, you know, because the Germans. But by then, I had those quite long black hair and black eyes. Of course, no makeup. That was-- and as I am olive-skinned, you know, and-- and as I am short, that is everything opposite to a Polish, you know, type.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in our side, which was more Ukrainian, it was not so strange, you, see, because the Ukrainian are the same-- dark hair. But when I walked into the room, nobody even took any notice. They just greeted me like I would-- like I would belong to them, you know? So that gave-- that gave me a little bit of assurance.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I was afraid. I-- by now, I was not afraid of anything. What I went through, and what I saw what is going on-- nothing could frighten me anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then my brother produced an identity card, and they were looking for ink. I had to put my fingerprints on it. I saw some commotion and this, but-- and then they produced ink.

speaker: interviewee
text: I put my fingerprints on it. It was blue ink-- which, again, was very important afterwards. And I put my fingerprints. And then at night, we left. My brother had a German pass, so we went by the German door. Which was, again, much better than by Polish-- but on the Polish side, somebody could recognize me. But so we went by the German door to the train. The train was full of German soldiers going to the east. And we traveled like that to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The night. No, not even the whole night. By-- by then, the Germans were speeding those, you know, supplies and soldiers to the eastern front.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went-- look, I had a coat on, a loose coat without a trace of fur. It was winter. It was very cold. I remember that my feet were freezing in this. But-- and that was a bit suspicious at this time, because only Jews did not have fur. The Germans confiscated all the scraps of fur-- fur coats, all the scraps of fur-- when they started the Russian offensive. And they were not expecting to be so long, until winter. They were not prepared for winter, you know? So they took from the Jews every scrap of fur.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we came to Lwow, and to make a long story shorter, my brother brought me to my eldest sister. She came back into my life. I told you that my elder sister married, and they were-- now I will tell you something more about my brother-in-law-- this brother-in-law. That brother-in-law--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Rosa's husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's Ella's husband, the eldest. Rosa's husband did not exist anymore at this time. Ella's husband was in the army, Polish army, quite a long time as an officer. And then they told him that if he wants to advance, he has to convert. As he did not want to do it, so he left the army. And he developed a very good position. He finished his study, and he-- they were in a small town more to the Ukraine on the-- in the east. He was the owner and director of a private high school. But when the war started, the army, the Polish army called up all the reserves. And they called him back with a much higher rank. So now he was a very high-ranking Polish officer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the-- Poland fell and the army disintegrated, he went back home. But he saw that it is a danger. First the Russian, and then the German, were looking for those high-ranking officers. On top of that, he was known in the town as a staunch supporter of the Polish government. He was a [INAUDIBLE] man, you see? So he knew that he has to take his family and run. And he was clever enough at the beginning, in '39, to change his name to a Polish name, to take Polish papers, and settle in Lwow as a Polish family.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my brother brought me to Lwow, he brought me to them. Luckily, we were not alike. Absolutely three different types. We had three different names. And we were just friends, you know? And that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So a few months was-- [LAUGHS] I'm going to the harder and harder part. So it was-- we passed Christmas all right. You lived with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with them. My brother was always on the way somewhere. He was a big businessman. He had something-- I got out from the ghetto-- the whole jewelry, you know? That was the base of his making all the businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pepa, before you go on, under what name were you at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this stage I had the name [? Maria ?] Yanviga Korolevich. Korolevich. Yes? And a young woman who, on the birth certificate, was younger than I was really. Seven years. But nobody ever gave me even this age. I looked so very young then. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But in the meantime, my niece, who was by then 12 years old-- she was-- she was exactly like my brother. Tall, blond, with those tresses, you know, very blond. Like a typical Polish that is blue-eyed. And she was brought up in a Catholic school in the small town. So she took me to a church. And she taught me a little bit about those-- the catechism, about the religion, about the rituals, and a little bit, you know, what she could-- a child like that could remember herself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After Christmas-- it was, I think, like, February-- two three months later, when I settled in, and the life started to get quite normal for us, I went somewhere with my brother. I don't remember where we were going, but, you see, I was never afraid. We had to go, we had to go. So I went. We were in the train in Lwow, and suddenly he told me, jump out. The German came to-- for a control. So I jumped out from the running train, not knowing why. I had my Kennkarte. Afterwards, he told me something is better that they don't see it. But he never told me what was wrong. When I was standing in front of my death, I-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Realized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I got to know what it was. But when-- then, it was too late to do something about it. Anyway, that time was everything all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: A couple of months later, I noticed that, you know, in all this turbulent times, we never looked at us, if our body functions normally or not, you know? And especially that I was never very regular. And then I noticed that it is a bit too late. It is three months, four months-- nothing happens.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my skirts started to get tight. So I told my-- my sister, I have to go to a doctor to make sure what's happening to me. She was panicking. She never went out. She was so afraid. But my brother-in-law took-- got a name of a gynecologist, and we went to see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue with the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape four of the interview with Pepa Rosenman. It's the 18th of December, 1995, and we are in Sydney, Australia.
